sources.notes: Statement and constraints: each operation must replace a current maximum-sum non-empty subarray; n,m <= 500 and total n,total m <= 500.
Luogu mirror used to cross-check the statement and constraints in Chinese.
Official editorial page checked; the tutorial body remained lazy-loaded, so detailed solution evidence came from other sources and self-derivation.
Used for the recursive maximum-subarray partition, threshold x, prefix DP with one exceptional piece, transition optimization, and operation reconstruction.
Re-derived why blocks below the operated-sum threshold remain unchanged, why at most one final piece may exceed x in maximum subarray sum, and why compress-to-x replay is valid.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu solution index did not expose readable solution text in this session, so it remains a candidate only.
Official editorial submission link; direct source access was blocked, so it was not used.

annotation.summary: Given arrays a and b, decide whether a can be transformed into b by repeatedly choosing any current maximum-sum non-empty subarray and replacing it by an arbitrary non-empty array, then construct such operations within the total replacement-length bound.
annotation.constraints: t <= 200; n,m <= 500 per test; total n and total m are each <= 500; values are bounded, so cubic preprocessing and DP are feasible.
annotation.core_idea: Build a canonical partition of a: for each interval choose a longest maximum-subarray interval and recurse on its left and right remainders. Later legal operations cannot cross these blocks. Guess x, the minimum sum among operated original blocks. Blocks with sum < x are never operated and must match the corresponding target slice exactly. Blocks with sum >= x can first be compressed, in decreasing block-sum order, into singleton [x]. Assign consecutive non-empty target pieces to the blocks; all but possibly one assigned piece must have maximum subarray sum <= x, because two pieces above x would make later maximum-subarray choices incompatible. For each x run f[i][j][used_big] over canonical blocks and target prefixes. Low-sum blocks use exact matching; high-sum blocks take bounded-max-subarray target intervals or the single unrestricted exceptional interval. Backtrack and replay compressions, normal replacements, then the exceptional replacement last.
annotation.complexity: O(n^3 + n^2*m + n*m^2) time and O(n^2 + m^2 + n*m) memory.
tricks: enumerate the minimum operated block sum x
blocks below x are forced unchanged
at most one final piece may have maximum subarray sum greater than x
construct by compressing operated blocks to singleton x before final replacements

solution_variants.name: threshold-max-subarray-partition-dp
solution_variants.summary: Precompute interval sums, maximum subarray sums, and exact prefix matches. Recursively partition a by longest maximum-subarray intervals. Enumerate x over block sums and run prefix partition DP with a one-bit exceptional-piece flag; backtracking gives target pieces and a valid operation order.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^3 + n^2*m + n*m^2) time and O(n^2 + m^2 + n*m) memory.

tags.evidence: The decisive feasibility check partitions the target prefix into consecutive pieces assigned to canonical source blocks.
Operation legality, the canonical source partition, and target-interval transition conditions all depend on maximum subarray sums.
Recursive longest maximum-subarray choices form a nested-or-disjoint interval skeleton that future operations cannot cross.
Enumerating x splits blocks into forced-unchanged sum<x blocks and replaceable sum>=x blocks with different transitions.
Stored DP predecessors plus compress-to-x ordering construct an explicit valid operation sequence.
Canonical source blocks are produced recursively by choosing a maximum-subarray interval and solving left and right remainders.
For fixed x, the farthest valid target endpoint moves monotonically as the left endpoint advances, optimizing transition scans.
